_doc: IO Profile Runner config — tuned for CLEAN gauge traces: (a) opt OUT of the async-completion kinds that flood the ring during readBurst, (b) longer workload for a richer time axis, (c) new gcChurn stage spins up Hashtables + LOH allocations so the GC gauges actually show activity.

database._doc: Larger cache than before (32 MB = 4096 pages at 8 KB/page) to reduce cache-miss pressure during readBurst so fewer completion records get generated in the first place.

spawnCheckpointLoop._comment: 20 iterations × 2K entities = 40K more entities, 20 ticks of mixed spawn+checkpoint activity

txFlood._comment: 12 ticks × 50 independent commits per tick = 600 commits. Each inner transaction opens, spawns one entity, commits, disposes — drives the Tx+UoW gauge's per-tick commit/created delta lines to a visibly-tall plateau across these ticks. Active-count area stays 0 (transactions close within the tick) — exercising the active signal requires a production-style workload with long-lived background transactions.

gcChurn._comment: 25 ticks of heavy managed-heap allocation — Hashtable fills (boxed int keys + int values), LOH arrays, short-lived strings. Drives Gen0/Gen1/Gen2/LOH gauge values visibly up and triggers real GC collections so GcStart/GcEnd + GcSuspension records appear.

transientChurn._comment: 8 × 1500 = 12,000 transient spawns across 8 ticks. At 112-byte stride / 71 chunks per 8 KB page, that's ~170 pages total — comfortably below the 1024-page cap raised via TransientOptions.PagesPerBlock=256. The gauge climbs in 8 visible steps and then flattens as the index-segment pool warms up.

readBurst._comment: Trimmed from 3 → 1 pass. With completion suppression below, the ring won't flood, but fewer passes still keeps the read cost bounded.

profile._doc: Opt-in set narrowed: we keep the SYNCHRONOUS kickoff spans (they're low-volume and useful) but DROP the async completion kinds because their count exploded during readBurst (160K+ completion records → ring overflow → dropped tail ticks). The kickoffs alone give us per-page latency via their durationTicks. Async-tail tracking can be re-enabled for targeted read-latency investigations by flipping the three completion flags back to true.
